chain_name: MLF → glycerol and lactic acid production → rounder mouthfeel

starting_factor.description: MLF performed by lactic acid bacteria produces not only lactic acid but also secondary metabolites that affect wine texture.

intermediate_steps.description: During MLF, lactic acid bacteria produce small amounts of additional glycerol, polysaccharides, and other complex molecules alongside lactic acid. These compounds have viscous, textural properties.

Lactic acid itself has a softer mouthfeel than malic acid. Lactic acid is associated with dairy products (yoghurt, cream) whereas malic acid is associated with tart fruit (green apple). This sensory difference is directly perceived as a rounder, creamier texture.

In conjunction with sur lie (lees contact), the combined effect of MLF-derived compounds and mannoproteins from yeast autolysis creates the characteristic rich, creamy, full-bodied mouthfeel of many white Burgundy and oaked Chardonnay styles.

final_outcome.description: Rounder, creamier, fuller mouthfeel. The wine has a perception of weight and softness on the palate beyond what alcohol or residual sugar alone would provide.

examples.description: Full MLF combined with extended lees contact produces the hallmark rich, buttery-textured Meursault even without the wine being perceptibly sweet or having high residual sugar.

100% MLF combined with new oak creates a full, round palate weight. When producers block MLF (e.g., some modern California producers seeking freshness), the wine is perceptibly leaner and more angular.

related_exam_questions: How does MLF affect the texture and mouthfeel of a white wine?
Why might a winemaker use both MLF and lees contact together?
Describe how winemaking choices can create a full-bodied white wine style.
distinction_note: A Distinction answer separates the acidity-softening effect (malic→lactic chemistry) from the textural contribution (glycerol, polysaccharides, lactic acid mouthfeel) and notes the synergy with lees contact.
